# UC-22.1.44 · GDPR Cross-Border Personal Data Flow Anomaly Detection

## Description

Sudden shifts in cross-border traffic can indicate misconfigured replication, insider misuse, or compromised accounts — each with transfer-law implications. This use case baselines bytes and session counts by destination country and alerts on statistically rare spikes for systems processing personal data.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Restrict baseline to GDPR-scoped internal sources via asset tags; (2) tune span and z-score thresholds by business unit; (3) alert SOC and privacy office on spikes to non-EEA countries; (4) integrate with TIA register to rule out approved bulk migrations; (5) retain investigation notes for regulators. Lookups: `gdpr_ropa_register`, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk App for Behavoral Profiling (optional).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CIM Network_Traffic, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`, `iplocation`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Restrict baseline to GDPR-scoped internal sources via asset tags; (2) tune span and z-score thresholds by business unit; (3) alert SOC and privacy office on spikes to non-EEA countries; (4) integrate with TIA register to rule out approved bulk migrations; (5) retain investigation notes for regulators.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out count as sessions
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-60d latest=now
  by All_Traffic.dest span=1d
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| iplocation dest
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name OUTPUT data_category
| where isnotnull(data_category)
| eventstats median(bytes_out) as med by Country
| eval z=round((bytes_out-med)/std(bytes_out),2)
| where abs(z) > 3
| table _time, dest, Country, bytes_out, z, sessions
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Cross-Border Personal Data Flow Anomaly Detection (Arts. 44-49)** — Sudden shifts in cross-border traffic can indicate misconfigured replication, insider misuse, or compromised accounts — each with transfer-law implications. This use case baselines bytes and session counts by destination country and alerts on statistically rare spikes for systems processing personal data.

Documented **Data sources**: CIM Network_Traffic, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv`, `iplocation`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk App for Behavoral Profiling (optional). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.

• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(data_category)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by Country** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **z** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where abs(z) > 3` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: _time, Country, bytes_out, dest, sessions, z.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (bytes baseline vs actual), Table (anomalies), Map (country), Single value (anomalies this week).

## SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out count as sessions
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-60d latest=now
  by All_Traffic.dest span=1d
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| iplocation dest
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name OUTPUT data_category
| where isnotnull(data_category)
| eventstats median(bytes_out) as med by Country
| eval z=round((bytes_out-med)/std(bytes_out),2)
| where abs(z) > 3
| table _time, dest, Country, bytes_out, z, sessions
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

## Visualization

Line chart (bytes baseline vs actual), Table (anomalies), Map (country), Single value (anomalies this week). Key fields: `Country`, `bytes_out`, `dest`, `sessions`, `z`.
